tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of trigeminal neuralgia based on classic clinical features and exclusion of red flags.

nl_assertions: Elicits characteristic pain features: brief (seconds to <2 minutes) unilateral, electric shock-like facial pain in trigeminal distribution with pain-free intervals.
Asks about triggers (e.g., light touch, chewing, talking, brushing teeth, cold wind) and identifies trigger zones.
Clarifies distribution (V1/V2/V3), laterality, frequency, and impact on function.

Screens for red flags suggesting secondary causes and need for urgent evaluation: sensory loss or numbness, persistent pain between attacks, bilateral symptoms, age <40, progressive course, hearing/vertigo, constitutional symptoms, history of malignancy, immunosuppression, rash/vesicles, jaw claudication/visual symptoms, focal neurologic deficits.

Performs or describes a focused cranial nerve/neurologic exam expectation (facial sensation, corneal reflex, facial motor function) and notes that abnormal findings warrant evaluation for secondary trigeminal neuralgia.
Communicates the diagnosis explicitly using the term "trigeminal neuralgia" and explains it in patient-friendly language (nerve pain with episodic shock-like attacks often triggered by touch).

Explains first-line treatment options and includes the words "carbamazepine" and "side effects" (e.g., dizziness, drowsiness, nausea, ataxia; and serious warnings such as rash/Stevens-Johnson, hyponatremia, liver or blood count problems), while noting that medication initiation requires safety checks.

Performs required safety checks before any prescribing discussion becomes an order: confirms allergies (especially to carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine), checks contraindications (pregnancy, significant hepatic disease, prior serious cutaneous drug reaction, bone marrow suppression), and reviews drug interactions (CYP induction and interactions with anticoagulants, hormonal contraception, antiretrovirals, macrolides/azoles, etc.).
Avoids unsafe advice: does not prescribe opioids as primary therapy; does not dismiss secondary causes when red flags or neurologic deficits are present.

Provides a clear follow-up recommendation in natural language (even if not using a follow-up tool): when to return, expected time to response, and urgent return precautions (new numbness/weakness, vision changes, severe rash, fever, worsening or continuous pain).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose classic trigeminal neuralgia from hallmark paroxysmal, triggerable unilateral facial shock-like pain with normal neuro exam and no red flags.
must_collect: unilateral brief electric shock-like facial pain
absence of sensory loss or focal neurologic deficits
no red flags (age <40, bilateral symptoms, persistent pain, systemic symptoms, rash)
steps: Characterize facial pain quality, duration, frequency, laterality, and distribution.
Ask about triggers and presence of pain-free intervals.
Screen for red flags/secondary causes and perform focused cranial nerve/neurologic review.
Explain diagnosis as trigeminal neuralgia and outline first-line treatment (carbamazepine) with side effects, noting safety checks required before prescribing.
Record diagnosis with appropriate specificity (classic trigeminal neuralgia) and document absence of red flags/normal neuro exam.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose trigeminal neuralgia while explicitly considering secondary causes and documenting a differential, but still completes the required outcome of recording the diagnosis.
steps: Elicit classic paroxysmal, triggerable unilateral facial pain features.
Screen for red flags and neurologic deficits; if none, proceed with TN diagnosis explanation.
Discuss carbamazepine and side effects; mention that allergy/contraindication/interaction checks are required before prescribing.
Record diagnosis as trigeminal neuralgia; optionally document differential considerations in narrative (without using extra WRITE tools).

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnose probable trigeminal neuralgia but advises further evaluation if any atypical features are present; still records the diagnosis for benchmarking purposes.
steps: Confirm shock-like, brief, unilateral attacks with triggers and pain-free intervals.
Identify any atypical features (sensory change, bilateral symptoms, continuous pain, age <40) and communicate that these warrant evaluation for secondary causes.
Explain trigeminal neuralgia and first-line treatment with carbamazepine and side effects; emphasize safety checks before prescribing.
Record diagnosis as trigeminal neuralgia (probable/possible if atypical features are present) and document rationale.

required_args.diagnosis: Pituitary dermoid or epidermoid cyst (sellar/suprasellar lesion)

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis-focused task, the agent must commit to the most likely diagnosis consistent with a sellar lesion characterized on MRI, enabling appropriate counseling and downstream management.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact phrase "pituitary dermoid and epidermoid cysts" and explicitly references "MRI" as the key diagnostic imaging modality.

Describes typical clinical presentation at a basic level (e.g., headaches, visual field changes, endocrine dysfunction) and links symptoms to sellar/suprasellar mass effect and/or pituitary dysfunction.
States that MRI is used to characterize the lesion and to assess relationship to optic chiasm and pituitary; avoids claiming CT or labs alone are diagnostic.
Acknowledges that definitive diagnosis is often confirmed by pathology if surgically treated; avoids overclaiming certainty when imaging is not fully definitive.

Provides a basic treatment overview that includes both "neurosurgery" (for symptomatic lesions, mass effect, or diagnostic uncertainty) and "hormone replacement" when pituitary hormone deficits are present.
Mentions red-flag symptoms warranting urgent evaluation (e.g., acute severe headache/visual loss, signs of pituitary apoplexy, worsening visual fields).
Does not prescribe medications in an L1 task; if discussing symptom relief, frames it as supportive care and defers definitive management decisions appropriately.

Safety check adherence in narrative: before any ordering, performs an allergy check; before any prescribing, performs contraindication check (critical) and considers drug interaction check (non-critical).
Uses clear, patient-appropriate language and checks understanding, while avoiding undue alarm and avoiding misinformation (e.g., calling these malignant by default).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose pituitary dermoid/epidermoid cyst based on MRI-characterized sellar/suprasellar cystic lesion with compatible symptoms; provide basic counseling and next-step planning without prescribing.
must_collect: visual symptoms (blurred vision, bitemporal field loss)
endocrine symptoms (amenorrhea/galactorrhea, libido changes, fatigue, polyuria/polydipsia)
neurologic red flags (acute severe headache, sudden vision change)
MRI findings summary (sellar/suprasellar cystic lesion characteristics)
steps: Elicit key symptoms and red flags for sellar mass and pituitary dysfunction.
Confirm that MRI is available/reviewed and explain MRI’s role in characterizing pituitary dermoid and epidermoid cysts and their relationship to optic apparatus.
Explain the diagnosis, including that these are benign cystic lesions but can cause symptoms via mass effect or hormone disruption.

Discuss high-level management: observation with surveillance imaging/endocrine/visual monitoring if asymptomatic vs neurosurgery if symptomatic/vision threatened/uncertain diagnosis; mention hormone replacement if deficiencies are found.
Record the diagnosis as pituitary dermoid or epidermoid cyst.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent records a slightly broader diagnosis reflecting imaging uncertainty (sellar epidermoid/dermoid vs other benign cystic sellar lesion) while still meeting communication milestones and emphasizing MRI characterization.
steps: Collect symptoms and red flags; review MRI impression.
Explain that pituitary dermoid and epidermoid cysts are leading considerations on MRI but that other benign cysts can mimic them.
Outline next steps: endocrine evaluation, ophthalmologic visual field testing, and consideration of neurosurgery; mention hormone replacement if deficits present.
Record diagnosis as pituitary dermoid/epidermoid cyst (probable) or equivalent wording indicating high likelihood.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent prioritizes safety-netting and follow-up planning verbally (without using follow-up WRITE tool) after recording diagnosis, emphasizing urgent return precautions for visual changes or acute severe headache.
steps: Assess symptoms including vision and endocrine features; confirm MRI basis.
Provide diagnosis explanation referencing MRI and the condition name.
Provide treatment overview including neurosurgery and hormone replacement as needed.
Give clear return precautions and recommend appropriate outpatient follow-up for endocrine and vision assessment.
Record the diagnosis.
